1: The angelic guide leads Ezekiel north to the chambers opposite the separate place in the outer court. The priestly chambers beside the holy complex locate the ministers of the sanctuary in proximity to their work. Christ creates a priestly order permanently settled near the divine presence: 'You are a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people for his own possession' (1 Peter 2:9). The church is not a visiting congregation but a resident priesthood.
2: The north chamber block: 100 cubits long and 50 cubits wide. The hundredfold length = complete; the fifty-cubit width corresponds to the Jubilee (year of release, Lev 25). The priestly chambers are both complete and Jubilary — the church's priestly residence is built on the dimensions of liberation. Christ proclaims 'the year of the LORD's favor' (Luke 4:19) and makes his priestly people permanent inhabitants of Jubilee.
3: The chambers face the twenty-cubit inner court and the pavement of the outer court. Situated between the inner court (near the Holy of Holies) and the outer court (open to more general access), the chambers occupy the mediating zone. Christ occupies this same mediating zone between God and humanity as the 'one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus' (1 Timothy 2:5) — exactly positioned at the intersection of the innermost holiness and the outer human court.
4: A one-cubit inner walk between the chambers, ten cubits wide. The inner walk of the priest through his own chambers — a private processional within the sanctuary complex. Christ has his own inner path: Hebrews 9:24 ('he entered not into holy places made with hands, a copy of the true things, but into heaven itself'). He also invites his priestly people to walk this inner way with him: 'Follow me' (John 1:43; Ezekiel 21:22) is the call to the priestly walk through the interior of his sanctuary.
5: The upper chambers were narrower than the lower because the galleries reduced the floor space at each story. The progression of narrowing toward the top — the closer to the divine presence, the more restricted the access — reflects the graduated holiness of the sacrificial system. Yet Christ inverts this narrowing: at the ascension he enters the narrowest holy place of all, the very throne of God, and then pours out the Spirit on all flesh (Acts 2:17), widening the access of God's presence from the uppermost point downward.
6: Three stories, without columns like the outer court, so the space was reduced from the lower and middle floors. The free-standing design of the priests' chambers — no borrowed support from outer-court pillars — mirrors the self-sufficient structure of the church's priestly life in Christ. Paul's letter to the Galatians insists that the Gentile church needs no pillars from Jerusalem (Galatians 2:6): the structure of the new covenant priesthood stands on Christ alone, not on borrowed external supports.
7: An outer wall fifty cubits long alongside the chambers. The outer wall creates the boundary of the holy area. Fifty cubits again evokes the Jubilee measurement. The Jubilee boundary is the boundary of the church: not a wall that excludes but a marker of the space within which the year of the LORD's favor operates. All within this boundary — all who are in Christ — live under the Jubilee release.
8: The chambers in the outer court measured fifty cubits, while those facing the nave measured one hundred. The doubled length of the inner-facing chambers over the outer-court chambers marks the greater depth of what opens toward the holy place. Access toward Christ deepens proportionally: the closer the orientation to the nave (to Christ's body), the greater the depth of the dwelling place that opens up. 'Abide in me, and I in you' (John 15:4) — the depth of the chambers grows as orientation toward the vine increases.
9: The entrance to the chambers from the east, as one enters from the outer court. East-entry again — the glory's direction, Christ's direction. The priestly chambers are entered from the same direction as the coming glory (Ezekiel 43). The church's priestly life is entered by turning toward Christ: baptism (Romans 6:3-4) is the east-facing entry into the priestly chambers of life in Christ.
10: Chambers along the south wall of the inner court, opposite the separate place. The southern positioning mirrors the northern chambers (Ezekiel 42:1-8), completing the bilateral layout. The church's priestly community surrounds the sanctuary on all sides — north and south. At Pentecost people from every direction of the compass heard the mighty works of God in their own language (Acts 2:9-11): the chambers of the priestly people of Christ open universally.
11: The south chambers matched the north in design and arrangement. Symmetry throughout the priestly complex. God's design for his priestly people has perfect bilateral symmetry: Jew and Gentile, male and female, slave and free — in Christ all are one (Galatians 3:28), all assigned to the same symmetric chambers of the priestly complex, all with the same access, all serving through the same one mediator.
12: The entrance to the south chambers matching the north: access via the eastern wall. Both sets of chambers entered from the east, from the direction of the coming glory. The consistent east-entry enforces the consistent principle: all priestly access to God is through Christ, who comes from the east, who is the way. 'I am the way, and the truth, and the life; no one comes to the Father except through me' (John 14:6) — one direction of entry for every chamber.
13: 'The north chambers and the south chambers which are opposite the separate place are the holy chambers where the priests who approach the LORD shall eat the most holy offerings — the sin offering, the grain offering, and the guilt offering.' The priests eat the most holy things in these chambers. Christ issues the same invitation to his royal priesthood: 'Unless you eat the flesh of the Son of Man and drink his blood, you have no life in you ... Whoever feeds on my flesh and drinks my blood abides in me, and I in him' (John 6:53, 56). The Eucharist is the priestly eating of the most holy offering in the chambers of the LORD's house — all three sacrifice-categories (sin, grain, guilt) fulfilled in the one holy food.
14: 'When the priests enter, they shall not go out of the holy place into the outer court without laying there the garments in which they minister ... for these are holy.' The priestly vestment change at the holy-to-outer transition. Christ gives his priestly people a robe of righteousness (Isaiah 61:10) to wear in the holy place, and the garment of salvation for the outer court of daily life. The distinction between the two is not abolished but maintained: the holiness of the priestly act of worship is particular and specific, while the garment of the saved one is worn everywhere. Revelation 7:14: 'They have washed their robes and made them white in the blood of the Lamb.'
15: 'Now when he had finished measuring the interior of the temple area, he led me out by the gate that faced east.' The measuring of the interior concludes, and the exit is through the east gate — the gate of the returning glory. The priestly tour of the inner courts ends where the Christological drama peaks: at the east gate, the gate through which Christ returns. The whole interior is measured and understood only in relation to the east-gate event — the coming of the glory that fills it.
16: The east side measured with the reed: 500 reeds. The measuring reed is six cubits (Ezekiel 40:5), so 500 reeds = 3,000 cubits. The fourfold 500-reed measurement creates a vast 1.5-kilometer-square enclosure. The scale of the sacred boundary exceeds any human city — Ezekiel's temple complex describes not a building but a transformed geography. The new Jerusalem scales this further: 12,000 stadia on each side (Revelation 21:16), the whole city a Holy of Holies. All geography is consecrated in Christ.
17: The north side measured 500 reeds. The four equal sides — north, south, east, west — describe a perfect square. The temple district is the new creation's basic shape: foursquare, comprehensive, equal in all directions. The gospel goes to the four corners (Matthew 28:19), and the result is a priestly community that is perfectly square — no corner neglected, no direction privileged. The fourfold equal measurement of the sacred district is the spatial form of Christ's universal dominion.
18: The south side measured 500 reeds. South is the direction of the nations in the OT cosmology (Arabia, Egypt). The 500 southern reeds include the nations in the sacred district's measurement. Paul's mission to the south and west (and beyond) is the measurement of Christ's foursquare dominion extending to the previously unconsecrated south: 'There is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither slave nor free' (Galatians 3:28) — all four cardinal peoples within the 500-cubit sacred square.
19: He turned about to the west side and measured 500 reeds. West = the direction of the nations, the sunset lands, the sea. Ezekiel's vision encompasses west as fully as east: no direction is excluded from the 500-reed measurement. The gospel's westward march (Acts 16:9, the Macedonian call; Romans 15:24, the planned mission to Spain) fulfills the westward reach of the sacred square. The setting sun's direction is claimed for the LORD's holy district.
20: 'It had a wall around it, 500 cubits long and 500 cubits broad, to make a separation between the holy and the common.' The enclosing wall that separates holy from common. Ephesians 2:14: 'For he himself is our peace, who has made us both one and has broken down in his flesh the dividing wall of hostility.' Christ breaks down the dividing wall between Jew and Gentile — but the wall of separation between the holy and the common is not destroyed; it is redrawn. The new boundary is Christ's atoning blood, which makes holy those who were common (1 Peter 2:10: 'Once you were not a people, but now you are God's people') and excludes whatever remains unclean (Revelation 21:27).
